content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 14, 2000


David A. DeBerry
City Attorney
City of Orange
300 East Chapman Avenue
Orange, CA 92866

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-00-188

Dear Mr. DeBerry:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Orange City
Councilmember Dan Slater regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act ("Act").¹ Since you have not asked about a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

If Councilmember Slater rented his residential property to city employees, would
the Act prohibit him from voting on union contracts affecting the salaries and benefits of
these employees?

CONCLUSION

The Act may prevent him from voting on union contracts if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the contract will increase his tenant's salary or benefits by $1,000 or
more during the term of the contract.

_________________________

¹ See Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2,
Sections 18109-18996 of the California Code of Regulations. The Commission is currently amending its
conflict of interest regulations. You may follow the progress of the changes on the Commission's website
(www.fppc.ca.gov).

² Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by formal written advice.
(Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. A-00-188
Page No. 2

FACTS

As part of his real estate business, Councilmember Slater owns several rental
properties within the City of Orange. A couple of city employees have asked to rent
residential property from Councilmember Slater.

Councilmember Slater would not be involved in any decision to hire or fire the
prospective renters. In addition, he would not be involved in hearing any disciplinary or
grievance appeals from these employees. However, he may vote on bargaining
agreements between the city and the union in which the subject employees are members.
These union contracts determine the salaries and benefits of the subject employees and
could result in increases or decreases in such salaries and benefits.

ANALYSIS

Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition

A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an
individual has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)

1. Is the requestor a public official?

The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section
87100.) As a member of the Orange City Council, Councilmember Slater is a public
official as defined by the Act. (Section 82048.)

2. Are the official's activities governed by the Act?

The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or
attempting to use one's official position to influence a governmental decision. (Section
87100.) By negotiating the terms of a labor contract between the city and the city
employees' union, Councilmember Slater will be "participating in making a
governmental decision." (Reg. 18702.2.) In addition, he will be "making a governmental
decision" if he votes to approve the contract. (Reg. 18702.1.)

3. What are the official's economic interests?

A public official has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,

File No. A-00-188
Page No. 3

distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official,³ or on the
following enumerated economic interests:

1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).

You have described one economic interest. Councilmember Slater is considering
renting residential property to city employees. Each city employee who rents from him
will be a source of income to him and therefore a potentially disqualifying economic
interest. (Section 87103(c).)

Once a public official identifies all relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of those economic interests. This determination takes
three steps, which we discuss as Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine
whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision.
(Reg. 18700(b)(4).) Based upon the type of involvement, the official must then find the
applicable materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(b)(5).)
After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Reg. 18700(b)(6).)

4. Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

A person is directly involved in a governmental decision if the person initiates, is
a named party in, or is the subject of, the decision. (Reg. 18704.1(a)(2).) A person is
"subject of the decision" if the decision involves a license, permit, or contract with that
person. (Ibid.) When a person is not directly involved in the decision, we consider that
person to be indirectly involved for purposes of finding the applicable materiality
standard. (Reg. 18704.1(b).)

³ For purposes of Section 87103, a decision will have a financial effect "on the official" if the
decision affects his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate
family. (Reg. 18703.5.) This is known as the personal financial effect rule. The rule does not appear to be
applicable to your facts. However, without knowing all of the material details regarding a specific contract,
we cannot fully address this issue.

File No. A-00-188
Page No. 4

Union contracts generally affect all city employees, or at least an entire class of
city employees. Therefore, we have advised that individual city employees are not
directly involved in the contract process. (King Advice Letter, No. I-99-145, copy
enclosed.)

5. What is the applicable materiality standard?

Commission Regulation 18705.3 sets forth the materiality standard for sources of
income who are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. Under the regulation, an
individual will be materially affected by a decision if, among other things, the decision
will affect the individual's income, investments, assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3).)

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?

A financial effect is reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is
made if it is substantially likely that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material
financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more than
a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Knowing the standards for materiality and foreseeability, we can now frame the
relevant question that Councilmember Slater must answer to determine whether he has a
conflict of interest. Will the decision to approve a union contract increase or decrease his
tenant's income by $1,000 or more? To answer this question, Councilmember Slater
must consider whether the proposed contract will increase his tenant's salary or benefits
by $1,000 or more during the term of the contract.

7. Does the public generally exception apply?

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision if the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "public generally"
exception. For this exception to apply, the decision must affect the official in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public.
(Reg. 18707.)

For individuals, a "significant segment" of the public generally is 5,000
individuals or 10 percent of the population of the official's jurisdiction or district. (Reg.
18707(b)(1).) Therefore, for the public generally exception to apply to your facts, the
decision must affect 5,000 individuals or 10 percent of the population of the City of
Orange in substantially the same manner as a tenant of the councilmember. A significant
segment may include non-union city employees and independent contractors hired by the

File No. A-00-188
Page No. 5

city if these individuals will be affected in substantially the same manner as the
councilmember's tenants. (Brooks Advice Letter, No. A-97-471.)

8. Does the Legally Required Participation Rule Apply?

The eighth step is the "legally required participation" rule. (Reg. 18708.) This
rule only applies when several public officials in the same agency have a conflict of
interest in the same decision. The rule does not appear to be relevant to your facts.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Julia Bilayer
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

the Act prohibit him from voting on union contracts affecting the salaries and benefits of
these employees?

CONCLUSION: The Act may prevent him from voting on union contracts if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the contract will increase his tenant's salary or benefits by $1,000 or
more during the term of the contract.

conflict of interest regulations. You may follow the progress of the changes on the Commission's website

(Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)
